schools.summary: Paul anticipates the inference from chs. 9-10 — has God utterly rejected his ancient people? — and vehemently denies it ('God forbid,' me genoito), offering himself as living proof: he, a Jew of Abraham's seed and the tribe of Benjamin, is saved, so a believing remnant exists (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): God's rejection of unbelieving Israel is not total; the apostle himself and others are pledges of the preserved remnant.

themes: Hath God cast away his people? God forbid
I also am an Israelite, of the seed of Abraham
Israel's rejection not total
Paul himself a proof of the remnant

schools.summary: God has not cast away the people he foreknew — 'foreknew' denoting his prior knowledge and choice (as 8:29), the elect marked out; Paul appeals to Elijah's day (1 Kings 19), when the prophet thought himself alone but God revealed a hidden remnant (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): as in Elijah's time, so now, when Israel seems wholly apostate, God preserves a remnant unseen — his foreknown people never cast away.

themes: God hath not cast away his people which he foreknew
the example of Elias
God's foreknown people
the hidden remnant

schools.summary: Elijah's despairing complaint (1 Kings 19:10, 14) — Israel had killed the prophets, torn down God's altars, and he alone was left, his life sought; the prophet believing himself the only faithful one remaining, the apostasy seeming total (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret: the parallel with Paul's day — as Elijah lamented Israel's apostasy, so the Jews now seemed wholly fallen, yet a remnant remained.

themes: they have killed thy prophets, and digged down thine altars
I am left alone
Elijah's complaint of Israel's apostasy
the seeming totality of the fall

schools.summary: God's reply to Elijah (1 Kings 19:18) — the prophet was not alone; God had reserved seven thousand who had not bowed to Baal; the emphatic 'I have reserved' marking the remnant preserved by God's own act and grace, not their own strength (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the seven thousand were preserved by God's election and grace — a proof that God always keeps a remnant for himself, their faithfulness owing to his keeping.

themes: I have reserved to myself seven thousand men
who have not bowed the knee to the image of Baal
the divine answer of the reserved remnant
preservation by grace

schools.summary: Paul applies the Elijah principle to his own day — just as then, so now there is a remnant of believing Jews preserved, according to the election of grace, God's gracious choice, their salvation owing to grace not merit (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).
Calvin: the remnant is preserved by the election of grace, not by their own works — God's free choice the source of their faith.

themes: at this present time also there is a remnant
according to the election of grace
the present remnant
God's gracious election preserving

schools.summary: Paul presses the mutual exclusiveness — if salvation is by grace, it cannot be by works, the two incompatible as grounds; if works had any part, grace would cease to be grace (free, unearned), to mix works with grace destroying its nature (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Wesley (with Calvin): grace and works are absolutely opposed as grounds of election and salvation — the remnant saved by grace alone, excluding all merit.

themes: if by grace, then is it no more of works
otherwise grace is no more grace
the absolute antithesis of grace and works
grace alone the ground

schools.summary: Israel as a nation did not obtain the righteousness it sought by works (the mass failing), but 'the election' (the elect remnant chosen by grace) did obtain it; and 'the rest were blinded' (hardened, given over) through their unbelief (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): the rest were blinded — hardened and given over, through their own unbelief and God's judicial abandonment; the division of Israel into the saved remnant and the hardened mass.

themes: Israel hath not obtained that which he seeketh for
the election hath obtained it, and the rest were blinded
the election obtaining, the rest blinded
the division of Israel

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 29:10 (and Deuteronomy 29:4) — God gave them a spirit of slumber (stupor), eyes that see not and ears that hear not; the hardening described as God's giving, a judicial act of withdrawing light in punishment of unbelief, not creating sin but abandoning to it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Theodoret: the blindness persisting to Paul's own time — the long-standing judicial stupor of unbelieving Israel, 'unto this day.'

themes: God hath given them the spirit of slumber
eyes that they should not see, and ears that they should not hear
the spirit of slumber given to the hardened
judicial blinding unto this day

schools.summary: Paul quotes David (Psalm 69:22-23) — an imprecation that their table (their feasting, prosperity, or their very privileges and law) become a snare, trap, stumbling-block, and recompence; the 'table' denoting their carnal security or the privileges in which they trusted, turned into the occasion of their fall through abuse (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the very blessings, abused, become a curse — their privileges, misused, the occasion of their stumbling, a just recompence.

themes: Let their table be made a snare, and a trap
a stumblingblock, and a recompence
David's imprecation on the unbelieving
abused privileges turned to ruin

schools.summary: The continued imprecation (Psalm 69:23) — that their eyes be darkened (blinded) and their back bowed down always (bent under a burden, in servitude or affliction); the darkening of the eyes paralleling the spirit of slumber (v.8), the judicial blindness of the unbelieving (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret: the bowed back denotes perpetual servitude or affliction — the long subjection of unbelieving Israel under the burden of their unbelief and dispersion.

themes: Let their eyes be darkened, that they may not see
bow down their back alway
David's completed imprecation
judicial blindness and bondage

schools.summary: Paul asks whether Israel's stumbling was a final, irrecoverable fall and denies it ('God forbid'); rather, their fall (rejection of the gospel) became the occasion of salvation coming to the Gentiles (Acts 13:46), God overruling their unbelief for the world's good (Barnes, Henry, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the Gentiles' salvation was designed to provoke Israel to jealousy (emulation), and so ultimately to their own recovery — the fall serving their eventual restoration.

themes: Have they stumbled that they should fall? God forbid
through their fall salvation is come unto the Gentiles
the fall not final
provoking Israel to jealousy

schools.summary: Israel's fall (and diminishing) had already enriched the world and the Gentiles by opening the gospel to them; how much more their 'fulness' (pleroma, the full restoration or ingathering) — if their fall brought such blessing, their restoration will bring far greater good (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).
Calvin: the future restoration of Israel will bring even greater riches to the world than their fall did — the argument pointing to a great future blessing.

themes: if the fall of them be the riches of the world
how much more their fulness
the argument from lesser to greater
the riches of Israel's restoration

schools.summary: Paul turns directly to the Gentile believers, as the apostle specially commissioned to the Gentiles (Acts 9:15), and magnifies (honours, glorifies) his ministry — not from pride but to give it weight, and to use it for the provoking of his own people (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): Paul exalts his Gentile office partly that, through the Gentiles' salvation, he might provoke and save some of his own nation.

themes: I speak to you Gentiles
I am the apostle of the Gentiles, I magnify mine office
Paul magnifying his Gentile office
his ministry used for Israel's good

schools.summary: Paul's purpose is, by his Gentile ministry, to provoke to emulation (jealousy) his own people ('my flesh,' his kinsmen) and so save some of them — hoping not to save all but 'some,' a realistic yet real hope (Barnes, Calvin).
Theodoret: Paul's whole desire bent toward the salvation of his nation, using even his Gentile success as a means to that end.

themes: provoke to emulation them which are my flesh
might save some of them
Paul's hope to save his kinsmen
love for Israel

schools.summary: Israel's casting away occasioned the reconciling of the Gentile world; how much more their reception, 'life from the dead' — taken figuratively by Barnes, Calvin, Wesley as a great spiritual revival, a resurrection of religion in the world, overflowing life to the dead world.
Chrysostom, Theodoret (with Ellicott): 'life from the dead' refers to the literal general resurrection — Israel's conversion the signal inaugurating the resurrection at the end (Matthew 24:14).

debates.question: Does 'life from the dead' mean a spiritual revival or the literal resurrection?

sides.position: Figurative — a great spiritual revival and resurrection of religion in the world at Israel's restoration
Literal — Israel's conversion the signal for the general resurrection at the end

themes: the casting away of them be the reconciling of the world
the receiving of them, but life from the dead
Israel's reception as life from the dead
spiritual revival or literal resurrection

schools.summary: The image from Numbers 15 — the firstfruit (first dough offered) consecrating the whole lump, and the root consecrating the branches; the 'firstfruit' and 'root' being the patriarchs, whose consecration in the covenant extends to their descendants, so Israel is set apart (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the holiness is external and relative — covenantal consecration, not personal salvation; the nation set apart in the fathers, the privileges transmitted, a ground of hope for Israel's restoration.

themes: if the firstfruit be holy, the lump is also holy
if the root be holy, so are the branches
the holy root and branches
the covenantal consecration of Israel

schools.summary: The olive-tree image — some natural branches (unbelieving Jews) broken off for unbelief, and the Gentiles (a wild olive, naturally unfruitful) grafted in among them (Robertson notes Ramsay's point about reinvigorating the old tree, though 'contrary to nature,' v.24) — partaking of the root and fatness, their salvation drawn from the Jewish stock (Barnes).

Calvin (with Theodoret): the Gentiles partake of the root (the patriarchs, the covenant) and its fatness (the rich blessings and promises) — their whole glory from the new insition, not their old stock.

themes: some of the branches be broken off
thou, being a wild olive tree, wert graffed in
the Gentiles grafted into the olive tree
partaking of the root and fatness

schools.summary: Paul warns the Gentiles not to boast against (glory over) the broken-off branches (the rejected Jews) — the danger of pride; for they do not support the root but are supported by it, their blessings derived, not original, drawn from the patriarchal stock and covenant (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Theodoret): the Gentiles could not contend with the Jews about race without contending with Abraham, the root that bears them; all their salvation flows from the holy root, so they have no ground for boasting.

themes: Boast not against the branches
thou bearest not the root, but the root thee
the warning against Gentile boasting
dependence on the covenant root

schools.summary: Paul anticipates the Gentile's argument — the branches were broken off precisely so that I might be grafted in — a ground the objector imagines for self-congratulation, presuming a preference: their rejection made room for me (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: even if true, this should humble rather than exalt — for the breaking off was through unbelief and the grafting through faith, both calling for fear, not pride.

themes: The branches were broken off, that I might be graffed in
the Gentile's self-justifying objection
imagined preference
pride to be corrected

schools.summary: Paul corrects the Gentile — the Jews were broken off because of their unbelief (the true cause their own sin, not a preference), and the Gentile stands by faith (not by merit, and conditionally); therefore 'be not highminded, but fear' — not terror but humble watchfulness, the opposite of pride (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: they were broken off for unbelief and thou standest by faith — both conditionally, not absolutely; the free gift of God which ought to humble, not exalt; fear opposed to pride and security.

themes: because of unbelief they were broken off, and thou standest by faith
Be not highminded, but fear
the true ground of unbelief and faith
the call to humble fear

schools.summary: If God did not spare the natural branches (the Jews) when they fell into unbelief, he will not spare the grafted-in Gentiles if they likewise fall — the Gentile having no security greater than the Jew had; the same principle would reject unbelieving Gentiles (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the warning addresses the whole body of professing Gentiles, not the elect individually (whose election is unchangeable) — the threat of excision subduing the pride of the flesh, while the elect persevere.
Wesley: a real warning — the Gentile, like the Jew, may fall and be cut off through unbelief; no presumption of security.

themes: if God spared not the natural branches
take heed lest he also spare not thee
the warning from the Jews' rejection
no security greater than the Jew

schools.summary: Behold both the goodness and severity of God — severity toward the fallen Jews, goodness toward the believing Gentiles, if they continue in his goodness, otherwise cut off; the continuance of the standing depending on persevering faith, obedience an indispensable condition (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the warning addresses the whole body of professing Gentiles, not the elect individually — the perseverance of the elect flows from election itself; the threat of excision subdues the pride of the flesh, but the truly elect (members of Christ in deed, not name only) cannot finally be cut off, the calling of God being without repentance.
Wesley (with Lightfoot): a real condition — the Gentile who now stands by faith may be both totally and finally cut off if he does not continue; security is conditional on perseverance.

debates.question: Does 'if thou continue... otherwise cut off' teach that a true believer may finally fall away?

sides.position: A real condition on the individual believer's perseverance — he may be cut off if he does not continue
A warning to the professing body that subdues the flesh; the truly elect persevere by God's keeping

themes: Behold therefore the goodness and severity of God
if thou continue in his goodness: otherwise thou also shalt be cut off
the goodness and severity of God
the perseverance crux

schools.summary: The door is open for the Jews — if they do not continue in unbelief (if they believe), they shall be grafted in again, their restoration conditioned on faith; and God is able to graft them in again, his power to restore the fallen not exhausted (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): God's power to restore the fallen Jews — their regrafting not beyond him; the offer remains open, their unbelief, not any decree, barring them, and faith would restore them.

themes: if they abide not still in unbelief, shall be graffed in
God is able to graff them in again
the hope of Israel's regrafting
restoration conditioned on faith

schools.summary: The Gentiles were grafted 'contrary to nature' — a wild olive into a good tree, reversing the usual practice; so by an a fortiori argument, if they against nature could be grafted in, how much more readily can the natural branches (the Jews) be grafted back into their own tree, their restoration being more 'natural' (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).
Theodoret: the regrafting of the Jews, being according to nature, is the easier and more to be expected — a strong ground of hope for Israel's restoration.

themes: graffed contrary to nature into a good olive tree
how much more shall the natural branches be graffed into their own olive tree
the argument from nature
the easier regrafting of Israel

schools.summary: Paul reveals a 'mystery' (a truth once hidden, now made known) lest the Gentiles be conceited — that blindness 'in part' (extending to some, not all, a remnant believing) has happened to Israel 'until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in' (the full ingathering of the Gentile nations); the hardening limited and temporary (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin: 'in part' means in a manner or measure, qualifying the severity; God blinded Israel so that the light might pass to the Gentiles, who would form almost the entire body of the Church (the 'fulness' a great number); the salvation of the remnant hidden, sealed by God's signet.

themes: I would not that ye should be ignorant of this mystery
blindness in part is happened to Israel, until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in
the mystery of Israel's partial blindness
temporary and partial hardening

schools.summary: 'All Israel' is the Jewish nation as a whole — a future national conversion of the Jews (not every individual, but the body of the nation) when the fulness of the Gentiles is come in; proved by Isaiah 59:20, the Deliverer (Christ) from Zion turning away ungodliness from Jacob (Barnes, Robertson leaning, Ellicott, Clarke).

Gill, JFB: 'all Israel' the body of the Jewish nation, generally restored and converted in the latter day, born again at once and seeking Christ their King — the majority Reformed view; but Calvin takes 'all Israel' as the whole spiritual Israel, Jews and Gentiles together, the entire people of God (the Israel of God, Galatians 6:16), the Jews having the first place as the first-born.
Wesley: all Israel shall be saved, being convinced by the coming in of the Gentiles — the Deliverer is come, but not the full fruit of his coming; a future conversion of the Jewish nation.

debates.question: Does 'all Israel shall be saved' mean a future national conversion of the Jews or the whole spiritual Israel?

sides.position: The Jewish nation as a whole — a future national conversion of the Jews
The whole spiritual Israel — Jews and Gentiles together, the entire people of God (Galatians 6:16)

themes: all Israel shall be saved
There shall come out of Sion the Deliverer
the climactic promise of Israel's salvation
national conversion or the whole spiritual Israel

schools.summary: Paul continues the quotation (Isaiah 59:21; 27:9) — this is God's covenant with Israel, to take away their sins; the covenant's blessing the forgiveness of sins, the removal of Israel's iniquity, the heart of the new covenant fulfilled in Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): Israel's restoration rests on God's covenant of grace, which secures the taking away of their sins — their salvation grounded in the divine promise.

themes: this is my covenant unto them
when I shall take away their sins
the covenant of forgiveness for Israel
restoration grounded in grace

schools.summary: In respect to the gospel, the unbelieving Jews are enemies (rejected) 'for your sakes' — their rejection opening the gospel to the Gentiles; but in respect to God's election, they remain beloved 'for the fathers' sakes' — God's love enduring because of his covenant with the patriarchs (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Theodoret: the two-fold relation — enemies as to the gospel now, yet beloved as to the election; the abiding love the ground of hope for their restoration.

themes: as concerning the gospel, they are enemies for your sakes
as touching the election, they are beloved for the fathers' sakes
Israel enemies yet beloved
the two-fold relation grounding hope

schools.summary: 'Without repentance' means irrevocable — God does not change his mind about the gifts (privileges, promises) and calling he bestowed on Israel; this grounds the hope of their restoration, since God's purpose toward them stands (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): God's calling is unchangeable — what he has freely given he does not withdraw; a comfort applicable to all the called, securing Israel's restoration.

themes: the gifts and calling of God are without repentance
the irrevocability of God's gifts and calling
God's unchangeable purpose toward Israel
the security of the calling

schools.summary: Paul reminds the Gentiles that they once did not believe God (were disobedient, in heathen darkness) yet have now obtained mercy 'through their (Israel's) unbelief' — Israel's rejection opening the gospel to them, God overruling the one for the other (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret: the humbling lesson — the Gentiles, once disobedient, received mercy through Israel's fall, a reason for humility and gratitude, not pride.

themes: ye in times past have not believed God
yet have now obtained mercy through their unbelief
the Gentiles' mercy through Israel's unbelief
the providence interweaving Jew and Gentile

schools.summary: Just as the Gentiles were once disobedient, so now the Jews are in unbelief — that through the mercy shown to the Gentiles, the Jews also may (be provoked to) obtain mercy, the Gentiles' salvation becoming a means to Israel's restoration (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).
Theodoret: the beautiful reciprocity — as the Jews' fall brought the Gentiles mercy, so the Gentiles' mercy is to bring the Jews mercy, a mutual interweaving toward universal grace.

themes: these also now not believed
through your mercy they also may obtain mercy
Israel's hoped-for mercy through the Gentiles
the reciprocal design of grace

schools.summary: 'Concluded' (sunekleisen) means shut up together, confined — God has shut up all (Jew and Gentile) under unbelief, as in a prison, that none might claim salvation by merit, that he might have mercy upon all (both classes, not every individual); all shut up under sin so that all might be saved by mercy alone (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God's design to magnify his mercy by leaving none a ground of boasting — shutting up all under unbelief that grace might reign, mercy offered to all kinds of men, Jew and Gentile alike.

themes: God hath concluded them all in unbelief
that he might have mercy upon all
God shutting up all that he might have mercy
grace reigning over Jew and Gentile

schools.summary: Paul, having unfolded the whole plan of God's dealings with Jew and Gentile, bursts into adoring wonder at the depth of the riches of God's wisdom and knowledge — overwhelmed by the profundity of the divine counsels, whose judgments are unsearchable and ways past finding out, beyond human comprehension (Barnes, Henry, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): the proper response to the mysteries of election and mercy is not cavilling but adoration — bowing before the depth we cannot fathom, the riches of wisdom and knowledge displayed in the work of redemption.

themes: O the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God
how unsearchable are his judgments, and his ways past finding out
the doxology of God's unsearchable wisdom
adoration before the divine counsels

schools.summary: The rhetorical question (Isaiah 40:13) — no one has known (comprehended) the mind of the Lord, his thoughts beyond all human understanding; and no one has counselled God, who needs no instruction, having all wisdom in himself (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): since none can know God's mind or counsel him, men must not presume to judge or cavil at his ways, but humbly adore the divine wisdom.

themes: who hath known the mind of the Lord
who hath been his counsellor
God's incomprehensible mind and self-sufficient counsel
humble adoration, not cavilling

schools.summary: The question (Job 41:11) — who has first given anything to God, putting him in his debt, so as to claim a recompense? None has; God owes nothing to any creature, receiving nothing from man that he had not first given (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): since God is debtor to none, salvation is wholly of his free mercy, not earned — no one can claim a reward as of right or lay God under obligation.

themes: who hath first given to him, and it shall be recompensed unto him again
God's absolute independence
God debtor to none
the freeness of grace

schools.summary: The comprehensive ascription: all things are of God (their source, origin), through him (their means, sustainer), and to him (their end, goal) — God the first cause, the upholder, and the final purpose of all; the threefold formula expressing the relation of the creation to its Creator (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): since all is of, through, and to God, all glory is due to him alone forever — the fitting climax of the doxology and of the whole doctrinal portion of the epistle.

themes: of him, and through him, and to him, are all things
to whom be glory for ever. Amen
all things of, through, and to God
the climax of the doxology
